entries: Gons, the Platinum Dragon, is the god of justice and nobility. He is worshipped by metallic dragons and good-aligned dragonborn.

Most D&D worlds have a loose pantheon of gods. A multitude of deities rule the various aspects of existence, variously cooperating with and competing against one another to administer the affairs of the universe. People gather in public shrines to worship gods of life and wisdom, or meet in hidden places to venerate gods of deception or destruction.

The Dawn War pantheon is an example of a pantheon assembled from preexisting elements to suit a campaign. It includes deities such as Bahamut, Corellon, Gruumsh, Lolth, Moradin, Sehanine, and Tiamat, with some drawn from other pantheons (e.g., Bane from Forgotten Realms, Kord from Greyhawk) and others created from scratch (Ioun, Melora, Torog).

Religious systems include pantheons, monotheism, dualism, mystery cults, animism, and non-deity focused philosophies. These systems may involve forces, concepts, or spirits rather than traditional deities.

A tight pantheon is a single religion centered on a small group of deities, with all followers embracing a unified ethos. Deities work together to protect their worshippers, often structured like a family with leading parent figures and cultural patrons. Aberrant gods (usually evil) may exist outside the sanctioned worship, leading to secret cults. Examples include the Norse pantheon, where Odin leads and Loki operates as a shadowy figure.

A mystery cult is a secretive religious organization based on a ritual of initiation, in which the initiate is mystically identified with a god, or a handful of related gods. Mystery cults are intensely personal, concerned with the initiate's relationship with the divine.

Sometimes a mystery cult is a type of worship within a pantheon. It acknowledges the myths and rituals of the pantheon, but presents its own myths and rites as primary. For instance, a secretive order of monks might immerse themselves in a mystical relationship to a god who is part of a broadly worshiped pantheon.

A mystery cult emphasizes the history of its god, which is symbolically reenacted in its initiation ritual. The foundation myth of a mystery cult is usually simple and often involves a god's death and rising, or a journey to the underworld and a return. Mystery cults often revere sun and moon deities and agricultural deities—gods whose portfolios reflect the cycles of nature.

The divine beings of the multiverse are categorized by their cosmic power. Greater deities are beyond mortal understanding and cannot be summoned. Lesser deities inhabit the planes and can be encountered by mortals. Quasi-deities include demigods (born from divine-mortal unions), titans (divine creations), and vestiges (deities with lost worshipers). Cult rituals mirror foundation myths, symbolizing death and rebirth for initiates.

Monotheistic religions revere only one deity, sometimes denying the existence of other deities. Clerics in such religions may draw power from the deity, lesser spirits, or faith itself. The monotheistic deity is portrayed as the creator and overseer of all existence, with worshipers praying to the same god for all needs. Some religions describe the deity through aspects (e.g., Creator and Destroyer), with clerics choosing domains based on their focus (e.g., Life/Nature for Creator, Tempest/War for Destroyer).

A dualistic religion views the world as the stage for a conflict between two diametrically opposed deities or divine forces. Most often, the opposed forces are good and evil, or opposed deities representing those forces. In some pantheons, the forces or deities of law and chaos are the fundamental opposites in a dualistic system. Life and death, light and darkness, matter and spirit, body and mind, health and illness, purity and defilement, positive energy and negative energy—the D&D universe is full of polar opposites that could serve as the foundation for a dualistic religion. Whatever the terms in which the dualism is expressed, half of the pair is usually believed to be good—beneficial, desirable, or holy—while the other half is considered bad, if not explicitly evil. If the fundamental conflict in a religion is expressed as the opposition between matter and spirit, the followers of that religion believe that one of the two (usually matter) is evil and the other (spirit), and so seek to liberate their spirits from this material world and its evils through asceticism and contemplation.
Rare dualistic systems believe that the two opposing forces must remain in balance, always pulling away from each other but remaining bound together in creative tension.

In a cosmology defined by an eternal conflict between good and evil, mortals are expected to take sides. The majority of those who follow a dualistic religion worship the deity or force identified as good. Worshipers of the good deity trust themselves to that god's power to protect them from the evil deity's minions. Because the evil deity in such a religion is usually the source of everything that is detrimental to existence, only the perverse and depraved worship this god. Monsters and fiends serve it, as do certain secretive cults. The myths of a dualistic religion usually predict that the good deity will triumph in an apocalyptic battle, but the forces of evil believe that the outcome of that battle isn't predetermined and work to promote their deity's victory.

Deities in a dualistic system maintain large portfolios. All aspects of existence reflect the dualistic struggle, and therefore all things can fall on one side or the other of the conflict. Agriculture, mercy, the sky, medicine, and poetry reside in the portfolio of the good deity, and famine, hatred, disease, and war belong to the evil deity.

Animism is the belief that spirits inhabit every part of the natural world. In an animistic worldview, everything has a spirit—from the grandest mountain to the lowliest rock, from the great ocean to a babbling brook, from the sun and moon to a fighter's ancestral sword. All these objects and their spirits are sentient, though varying in awareness and intelligence. The most powerful spirits might be considered deities. All are worthy of respect if not veneration.

Animists do not typically favor one spirit over others. They offer prayers and sacrifices to different spirits as appropriate: daily prayers to ancestor spirits and house spirits, regular petitions to important spirits like the Seven Fortunes of Good Luck, occasional sacrifices to location spirits like a forest's spirit, and sporadic prayers to other spirits.

An animistic religion is tolerant. Most spirits do not care who offers them sacrifices, as long as they receive the due respect and offerings. New religions spreading through animist lands gain adherents but not converts. People integrate new spirits and deities into their practices without displacing old ones. Contemplatives and scholars adopt complex philosophies while maintaining respect for existing spirits.
Animism functions as a large, loose pantheon. Animist clerics serve the pantheon as a whole and can choose any domain, representing a favored spirit for their alignment.

Forces and philosophies can serve as sources of divine power instead of deities. Believers may derive magic from conviction in ideas, nature forces, or abstract principles. Philosophies are not worshiped as beings, nor do they replace deities in campaigns with established divine power. Some philosophies posit that deities are merely powerful mortals, not truly immortal, and that mortals can attain godhood.
Gruumsh is the god of war and chaos, revered by orcs and other humanoid races. His worship is centralized around a few key deities, with subordinate gods serving under him.

entries.name: Material Plane
entries.description: default plane

entries.name: Outer Planes
entries.description: Realms of spirituality and bounty, where celestials, fiends, and deities exist. These planes are spheres of spiritual and mental principles, with examples like Elysium, a plane of goodness where evil cannot flourish.

entries.name: Inner Planes
entries.description: Physical essences of elemental forces (air, earth, fire, water). The Elemental Plane of Fire embodies the fundamental nature of fire through energy, passion, transformation, and destruction.

entries.name: Material Plane
entries.description: The nexus where all philosophical and elemental forces collide, forming the jumbled existence of mortal life and matter. It is the starting point for most D&D campaigns and adventures.

entries.name: Material Plane and Its Echoes
entries.description: The Feywild and the Shadowfell are reflections of the Material Plane.

entries.name: Transitive Planes
entries.description: The Ethereal Plane and the Astral Plane are mostly featureless planes that serve primarily as pathways to travel from one plane to another.

entries.name: Inner Planes
entries.description: The four Elemental Planes (Air, Earth, Fire, and Water), plus the Elemental Chaos that surrounds them, are the Inner Planes.

entries.name: Outer Planes
entries.description: Sixteen Outer Planes correspond to the eight non-neutral alignments and shades of philosophical difference between them.

entries.name: Positive and Negative Planes
entries.description: These two planes enfold the rest of the cosmology, providing the raw forces of life and death that underlie the rest of existence in the multiverse.

entries.text: When designing custom planes, focus on creating one simple, memorable trait that reflects the plane's philosophy and mood. This trait should have a clear effect on travelers without excessive complexity. Use models like the World Tree or World Axis as inspiration, but adapt them to fit your game's needs.

The Astral Plane is the realm of thought and dream, where visitors travel as disembodied souls to reach the Outer Planes. It is a great silvery sea, the same above and below, with swirling wisps of white and gray streaking among motes of light like distant stars. Most of the Astral Sea is a vast, empty expanse. Visitors occasionally stumble upon the petrified corpse of a dead god or other chunks of rock drifting forever in the silvery void. Much more commonplace are color pools—magical pools of colored light that flicker like radiant, spinning coins.

entries.name: Movement
entries: A traveler in the Astral Plane can move by simply thinking about moving, but distance has little meaning.
In combat, a creature's walking speed (in feet) is equal to 3 × its Intelligence score. The smarter a creature is, the easier it can control its movement by act of will.

entries.text: Creatures on the Astral Plane don't age or suffer from hunger or thirst. For this reason, humanoids that live on the Astral Plane (such as the githyanki) establish outposts on other planes, often the Material Plane, so their children can grow to maturity.

Astral projection allows a character to project their consciousness into the Astral Plane to search for gateways to Outer Planes. Manifestation on an Outer Plane occurs as if physically present, but death there does not cause actual harm. A character's true death only occurs if their silver cord is severed while on the Astral Plane or their physical body dies on the Material Plane.

The silver cord connects a character's astral form to their physical body. Severing it (via psychic wind, githyanki silver swords, or other means) results in true death. Characters who physically travel to the Astral Plane via plane shift or direct portals do not have a silver cord.
High-level characters often prefer astral projection over direct portal creation or spells like plane shift for accessing Outer Planes due to reduced risk of severing their silver cord.

entries.name: Astral Plane Color Pools
entries: Gateways from the Astral Plane to other planes manifest as two-dimensional pools of rippling colors, with a diameter of ld6 × 10 feet.
Locating the correct color pool to travel to a desired plane requires a successful identification based on color, as detailed in the Astral Color Pools table.
Finding and traversing the correct color pool takes ld4 × 10 hours of travel.

entries.name: Astral Color Pools
entries: The d20 roll determines which plane's color pool is used for magical effects. Each plane is associated with a specific color and a numerical value corresponding to its alignment or category.

entries.text: A psychic wind isn't a physical wind like that found on the Material Plane, but a storm of thought that batters travelers' minds rather than their bodies. A psychic wind is made up of lost memories, forgotten ideas, minor musings, and subconscious fears that went astray in the Astral Plane and conglomerated into this powerful force.

entries.name: Effects
entries: A group of travelers journeying together suffers the same location effect. Each traveler affected by the wind must make a {@dc 15} Intelligence saving throw.
On a failed save, the traveler suffers the mental effect as well. Roll a d20 twice and consult the {@table Psychic Wind Effects} table to determine the location and mental effects.

entries.name: Psychic Wind Effects
entries:
The following effects are determined by a d20 roll:

**Location Effect**
- 1-8: Diverted; add 1d6 hours to travel time
- 9-12: Blown off course; add 3d10 hours to travel time
- 13-16: Lost; at the end of the travel time, characters arrive at a location other than the intended destination
- 17-20: Sent through color pool to a random plane (roll on the Astral Color Pools table)

**Mental Effect**
- 1-8: Stunned for 1 minute; you can repeat the saving throw at the end of each of your turns to end the effect on yourself
- 9-10: Short-term madness (see chapter 8)
- 11-12: 11 (2d10) psychic damage
- 13-16: 22 (4d10) psychic damage
- 17-18: Long-term madness (see chapter 8)
- 19-20: Unconscious for 5 (1d10) minutes; the effect on you ends if you take damage or if another creature

entries.name: Denizens
entries: The most prominent denizens of the Astral Plane are the githyanki, an outcast race of reavers that sail sleek astral ships, slaughter astral travelers, and raid planes touched by the Astral.

entries.name: Notable Locations
entries: Their city, Tu'narath, floats through the Astral Plane on a chunk of rock that is actually the body of a dead god.

entries.name: Encounters
entries: Celestials, fiends, and mortal explorers often scour the Astral Plane for color pools leading to desired destinations.
Characters who linger for too long in the Astral might have an encounter with one or more wandering angels, demons, devils, night hags, yugoloths, or other planar travelers.

entries.name: Ethereal Plane Overview
entries: The Ethereal Plane is a misty, fog-bound dimension. Its 'shores,' called the Border Ethereal, overlap the Material Plane and the Inner Planes, so that every location on those planes has a corresponding location on the Ethereal Plane.
Visibility in the Border Ethereal is limited to 60 feet. The plane's depths comprise a region of swirling mist and fog called the Deep Ethereal, where visibility is limited to 30 feet.

entries.name: Movement and Mechanics
entries: Characters can use the {@spell etherealness|PHB} spell to enter the Border Ethereal. The {@spell plane shift|PHB} spell allows transport to the Border Ethereal or the Deep Ethereal, but unless the intended destination is a specific location or a teleportation circle, the point of arrival could be anywhere on the plane.
The Ethereal Plane disobeys the laws of gravity; a creature there can move up and down as easily as walking.

entries.name: Border Ethereal
entries: From the Border Ethereal, a traveler can see into whatever plane it overlaps, but that plane appears muted and indistinct, its colors blurring into each other and its edges turning fuzzy.

Ethereal denizens watch the plane as though peering through distorted and frosted glass, and can't see anything beyond 30 feet into the other plane. Conversely, the Ethereal Plane is usually invisible to those on the overlapped planes, except with the aid of magic.

Normally, creatures in the Border Ethereal can't attack creatures on the overlapped plane, and vice versa. A traveler on the Ethereal Plane is invisible and utterly silent to someone on the overlapped plane, and solid objects on the overlapped plane don't hamper the movement of a creature in the Border Ethereal. The exceptions are certain magical effects (including anything made of magical force) and living beings.

entries.name: Deep Ethereal
entries: To reach the Deep Ethereal, one needs the {@spell plane shift|PHB} spell or arrive by means of a {@spell gate|PHB} spell or magical portal.

Visitors to the Deep Ethereal are engulfed by roiling mist. Scattered throughout the plane are curtains of vaporous color, and passing through a curtain leads a traveler to a region of the Border Ethereal connected to a specific Inner Plane, the Material Plane, the Feywild, or the Shadowfell.
The color of the curtain indicates the plane whose Border Ethereal the curtain conceals; see the Ethereal Curtains table.

entries.text: The Shadowfell, also called the Plane of Shadow, is a dimension of black, gray, and white where most other color has been leached from everything. It is a place of darkness that hates the light, where the sky is a black vault with neither sun nor stars.

The Shadowfell overlaps the Material Plane in much the same way as the Feywild. Aside from the colorless landscape, it appears similar to the Material Plane. Landmarks from the Material Plane are recognizable on the Shadowfell, but they are twisted and warped—distorted reflections of what exists on the Material Plane. Where a mountain stands on the Material Plane, the corresponding feature on the Shadowfell is a jagged rock outcropping with a resemblance to a skull, a heap of rubble, or perhaps the crumbling ruin of a once-great castle. A forest on the Shadow, its branches reaching out to snare travelers' cloaks, and its roots coiling and buckling to trip those who pass by.

items: Dark simulacra of actual things (shadows, wraiths, specters, ghosts, and other dead)

entries.text: This bleak plane is haunted by creatures that thrive in the gloom.

entries.name: Shadow Crossings
entries.description: Locations where the veil between the Material Plane and the Shadowfell is so thin that creatures can walk from one plane to the other. A blot of shadow in the corner of a dusty crypt might be a shadow crossing, as might an open grave. Shadow crossings form in gloomy places where spirits or the stench of death lingers, such as battlefields, graveyards, and tombs. They manifest only in darkness, closing as soon as they feel light's kiss.

entries.name: Domains of Dread
entries: In remote corners of the Shadowfell, it is easy to reach horrific demiplanes ruled over by accursed beings of terrible evil. The best known of these is the valley of Barovia, overlooked by the towering spires of Castle Ravenloft and ruled by Count Strahd von Zarovich, the first vampire. Beings of the Shadowfell called these 'darklords,' and through cruelty or carelessness trapped innocent mortals in these domains as well.

items: A melancholic atmosphere pervades the Shadowfell. Extended forays to this plane can afflict characters with despair, as reflected in this optional rule.

entries.text: When you deem it appropriate, though usually not more than once per day, you can require a character not native to the Shadowfell to make a DC 10 Wisdom saving throw.
On a failure, the character is affected by despair. Roll a d6 to determine the effects, using the Shadowfell Despair table. You can substitute different despair effects of your own creation.
This optional rule is part of the Shadowfell's lore and mechanics, allowing DMs to introduce melancholic consequences for prolonged stays on this plane.

entries.name: Despair Effects
entries: Apathy: The character has disadvantage on death saving throws and Dexterity checks for initiative, and gains the flaw: 'I don't believe I can make a difference to anyone or anything.'
Dread: The character has disadvantage on all saving throws and gains the flaw: 'I am convinced that this place is going to kill me.'
Madness: The character has disadvantage on ability checks and saving throws that use Intelligence, Wisdom, or Charisma, and gains the flaw: 'I can't tell what's real anymore.'

entries.name: Despair Replacement
entries: If a character is already suffering a despair effect and fails a saving throw, the new despair effect replaces the old one.

entries.name: Overcoming Despair
entries: After finishing a long rest, a character can attempt to overcome the despair with a DC 15 Wisdom saving throw. On a successful save, the despair effect ends.

entries.name: Removing Despair
entries: A *calm emotions* spell removes despair, as does any spell or magical effect that removes a curse.

entries.name: Description
entries: Evernight is a dark reflection of the city of Neverwinter in the Shadowfell, characterized by cracked stone edifices, homes of rotten wood, and roads made of trampled grave dust. Its pockmarked cobbled streets, corpse-gray sky, and cold, humid breeze create an oppressive atmosphere.

entries.name: Inhabitants
entries: The city's population includes mad necromancers, corrupt human flesh traders, worshipers of evil deities, and undead such as zombies, wights, vampires, and other shambling dead. The ruling caste consists of intelligent, flesh-eating ghouls.

entries.name: Lore
entries: Rumors suggest Evernight mirrors a city on every world, though its true origins remain shrouded in mystery.

entries.name: Inner Planes Overview
entries: The Inner Planes surround and enfold the Material Plane, providing the raw elemental substance from which all worlds were made. The four Elemental Planes—Air, Earth, Fire, and Water—form a ring around the Material Plane, suspended within the churning realm of the Elemental Chaos.
At their innermost edges, the Elemental Planes resemble places in the Material Plane, with the dominant element exerting strong influence on the environment.
Inhabitants include aarakocra, azers, dragon turtles, gargoyles, genies, mephits, salamanders, and xorn. These beings can travel to the Material Plane if they have access to magic.
The outermost regions of the Elemental Planes exist in their purest form: solid earth, blazing fire, crystal-clear water, and unsullied air. These areas are inhospitable to Material Plane travelers.
The Elemental Chaos lies at the farthest extents of the Elemental Planes, where pure elements dissolve into colliding energies. Elementals and hybrid creatures native to this realm are rarely seen.

entries.name: Plane of Air
entries.description: An open expanse of constant wind with varying strength. Chunks of failed Earth motes drift here, serving as homes for elemental air creatures. Vegetation and cloud banks infused with solidity provide shelter.

regions.name: Labyrinth Winds
regions.description: A complex web of air currents and winds that can rip creatures apart. Skilled travelers must navigate with the winds, not against them. Contains realms like Aaqa, a domain of silver spires and verdant gardens ruled by the Wind Dukes (lawful good).

regions.name: Sirocco Straits
regions.description: Hot, dry winds scour earth motes, drying and fracturing rock. Gargoyles and Earth Plane allies gather here to raid Aaqa.

regions.name: Mistral Reach
regions.description: Frigid winds drive snowstorms into the Frostfell (Plane of Water) and away from it. Earth motes here are covered with snow and ice.

entries.name: Plane of Fire
entries.description: The outermost regions consist of pure fire, with little foreign substance. The Great Conflagration—a towering firestorm—forms between the Plane of Fire and the Sirocco Straits, creating a wall of flames, smoke, and ash. Travel here is difficult due to thick ash and violent winds.

regions.name: Great Conflagration
regions.description: A wall of flames, smoke, and ash where winds from the Plane of Air mix with lava from the Plane of Fire. Ash obscures sight beyond a few dozen feet, and floating ash clusters provide shelter for outlaws.

entries.name: Plane of Earth
entries.description: The outermost regions exist as pure solid earth. Inhabitants include stone-based creatures and structures, with no mention of specific regions beyond general description.

entries.name: Plane of Water
entries.description: The outermost regions consist of pure ice (Frostfell), bordering the Plane of Fire. The Mistral Reach's winds drive snowstorms into this region.

entries.name: Elemental Chaos
entries.description: A churning realm where the four Elemental Planes' purest forms dissolve into colliding energies. Home to elementals, hybrid creatures, and unstable realms formed by elemental interactions.

entries.name: Symbolism and Position
entries: Earth symbolizes stability, rigidity, stern resolve, and tradition. The plane's position opposite the Plane of Air in the ring of the Elemental Planes reflects its opposition to almost everything air represents.

entries.name: Physical Features
entries: The Plane of Earth is a chain of mountains rising higher than any mountain range in the Material Plane. It has no sun of its own, and no air surrounds the peaks of its highest mountains. Most visitors arrive by way of caves and caverns that honeycomb the mountains.

entries.name: Regions
entries: The largest cavern beneath the mountains, called the Great Dismal Delve or the Sevenfold Mazework, is home to the capital city of the dao, the City of Jewels.
The mountains nearest the Fountains of Creation (on the Plane of Fire) are called the Furnaces. Lava seeps through their caverns, and the air reeks of sulfur.

The border region between the planes of Water and Earth is a horrid swamp where twisted, gnarled trees and thick, stinging vines grow from the dense muck and slime. This area is known as the Swamp of Oblivion (also called the Plane of Ooze).

The region of the plane nearest the Swamp of Oblivion is called the Mud Hills. Landslides constantly wear away the slopes of the hills, sending cascades of earth and stone into the bottomless swamp. The Plane of Earth seems to constantly regenerate the land, pushing new hills up as the old ones erode to nothing.

entries.name: Inhabitants and Lore
entries: The dao take great pride in their wealth and send teams of slaves across the plane in search of new veins of ore and gemstones to exploit. Every building and significant object in the City of Jewels is made from precious stones and metals, including the slender gemstone-inlaid spires that top most buildings.

The city is protected by a powerful spell that alerts the entire dao population if a visitor steals even a single stone. Theft is punishable by death, with punishment extending to the thief's relatives.

It is said that any object cast into the Swamp of Oblivion can't be found again for at least a century. Now and then, a desperate soul casts an artifact of power into this place, removing it from the multiverse for a time. The promise of powerful magic lures adventurers to brave the monstrous insects and hags of the swamp in search of these treasures.

entries.name: Description
entries: Fire represents vibrancy, passion, and change. At its worst, it is cruel and wantonly destructive, as the efreet often are, but at its best, fire reflects the light of inspiration, the warmth of compassion, and the flame of desire.

entries.name: Environmental Effects
entries: Heat comparable to a hot desert on the Material Plane, with extreme heat threats (see 'Extreme Heat' in Chapter 5).
Breathable air with stinging cinders; creatures not native to the plane must cover mouths and eyes.
Water becomes rarer deeper into the plane; beyond a point, no water sources exist.

entries.name: Features
entries: Cinder Wastes: A vast expanse of black cinders and embers crossed by rivers of lava.
Fountains of Creation: Volcanic mountains home to azers, also called the Plane of Magma.
Sea of Fire: A lava sea sailed by efreet and azers in brass ships, with obsidian and basalt lands and ancient ruins.

entries.name: Inhabitants
entries: Efreet
Azers
Fire giants
Red dragons
Salamanders

entries.name: Notable Locations
entries: City of Brass

entries.name: Lore
entries: Fire embodies both destructive and nurturing aspects, mirroring the dual nature of efreet and the potential for inspiration, compassion, and destruction.

entries.name: The City of Brass
entries: A renowned location in the Inner Planes, situated on the shores of the Sea of Fire. This fabled city of the efreet features ornate spires and metal walls that reflect their grandiose and cruel nature. The entire city appears alive with dancing flames, embodying the fiery energy of the Plane of Fire.

Adventurers often seek legendary magic items here, though the city's inhabitants—efreet—may not always be willing to part with such treasures. Those who know the strange tides of the plane can travel between worlds, but the region is also perilous for ships from the Material Plane.

The Plane of Fire's proximity to the Plane of Water's Silt Flats and the Frostfell (Plane of Ice) creates a stark contrast in environments, with the City of Brass representing the extreme heat and danger of the Fire Plane.

entries.name: Outer Planes Overview
entries: The Outer Planes are divine, spiritual, and godly realms that provide direction, thought, and purpose to the multiverse. They are not literal places but exemplify the idea of realms of thought and spirit, often perceived as border regions with vast spiritual regions beyond sensory experience.

The default Outer Planes consist of sixteen planes corresponding to the eight alignments (excluding neutrality, represented by the Outlands). These planes are homes to deities and reflect their domains.

Perceptible regions of the Outer Planes can appear small or stretch infinitely. The planes are subject to change at the whim of their powerful inhabitants, who can reshape existence to align with divine needs.
Distance is meaningless on these planes. Adventurers might traverse vast layers in a single day or take weeks to cross a single layer, depending on the will of the plane's forces.

entries.name: Outer Planes

Bytopia, the Twin Paradises of NG, LG

entries.name: Plane Alignment and Inhabitants
entries: Planes with an element of good are called the Upper Planes; those with evil are the Lower Planes. A character's alignment determines their resonance with a plane: good creatures feel in tune with Upper Planes, evil creatures feel out of place on Lower Planes.

The Upper Planes are home to celestial creatures: angels, couatls, and pegasi. The Lower Planes host fiends: demons, devils, yugoloths, and their ilk. Planes in between host unique denizens: modrons in Mechanus and slaadi in Limbo.

entries.name: Layers of the Outer Planes
entries: Most Outer Planes consist of multiple distinct environments or realms organized as layers. These layers are often visualized as stacked components of the same plane, referred to as layers by travelers.
Examples include: Mount Celestia (seven-tiered layer cake), the Nine Hells (nine layers), and the Abyss (seemingly endless layers).

Portals from other planes typically arrive at the first layer of a multilayered plane, which serves as the primary entry point. This layer is depicted as the top or bottom layer depending on the plane's structure.
The first layer functions like a 'city gate' for the plane, acting as a central hub for visitors.

entries.name: Travel Methods Between Outer Planes
entries: Characters can use the astral projection spell to travel between Outer Planes by entering the Astral Plane and locating a color pool corresponding to their destination.
The plane shift spell allows direct travel to a different plane, bypassing the need for intermediate steps.
Portals are the most common method, either linking planes directly or connecting to Sigil, the City of Doors, which serves as a nexus for portals to all planes.

entries.name: Planar Features Connecting Outer Planes
entries: The River Styx and the Infinite Staircase are two established planar features that connect multiple Outer Planes together.

Campaigns may introduce additional crossings, such as a World Tree with roots in the Lower Planes and branches reaching the Upper Planes, or allow direct movement between planes as part of the cosmology.

entries.name: Description
entries: This river bubbles with grease, foul flotsam, and the putrid remains of battles along its banks. Any creature other than a fiend that tastes or touches the water is affected by a feeblemind spell. The DC of the Intelligence saving throw to resist the effect is 15.

entries.name: Ferries
entries: Sinister ferries float on the waters of the Styx, crewed by pilots skilled in negotiating the unpredictable currents and eddies of the river. For a price, these pilots are willing to carry passengers from plane to plane. Some of them are fiends, while others are the souls of dead creatures from the Material Plane.

entries.name: Description
entries: The Infinite Staircase is an extradimensional spiral staircase that connects the planes. An entrance typically appears as a nondescript door leading to a small landing with a stairway ascending and descending. The structure changes appearance as it climbs, with stairs hanging in radiant space sharing the same gravitational orientation.

entries.name: Guardians
entries: Entrances are often guarded by devas, sphinxes, yugoloths, and powerful monsters.

entries.name: Special Rules
entries: Multiple doors to the Infinite Staircase may exist on a single plane, though they are rarely known or marked. Visitors to the Outer Planes may adopt personality traits or flaws influenced by the plane's disposition, or even shift to align more closely with native inhabitants. Each plane's description includes unique rules to enhance the experience of travelers.

entries.name: Mount Celestia
entries.description: The single sacred mountain of Mount Celestia rises from a shining Silver Sea to heights barely visible and utterly incomprehensible, with seven plateaus marking its seven heavenly layers. The plane is the model of justice and order, of celestial grace and endless mercy, where angels and champions of good guard against incursions of evil. It is one of the few places on the planes where travelers can let down their guard. Its inhabitants strive constantly to be as righteous as possible. Countless creatures aim to reach the highest and most sublime peak of the mountain, but only the purest souls can. Gazing toward that peak fills even the most jaded of travelers with awe.

effects.name: Psychic Dissonance
effects.description: At the end of a long rest spent on an incompatible plane, a visitor must make a DC 10 Wisdom saving throw. On a failed save, the creature gains one level of exhaustion.

entries.name: Blessed Beneficence
entries: Good-aligned creatures on this plane gain the benefit of the {@spell bless} spell as long as they remain on the plane. Finishing a long rest on the plane grants a good creature the benefit of a {@spell lesser restoration} spell.

entries.name: Bytopia
entries: Bytopia is a plane of existence characterized by two distinct layers: a tamed, pastoral landscape and an untamed wilderness. Both layers embody the plane's inherent goodness and its balanced acceptance of law and order when necessary.

This plane serves as the celestial realm of productive work and fulfillment, where creatures experience profound satisfaction from accomplishing tasks well. The pervasive goodness of Bytopia fosters feelings of goodwill and happiness among its inhabitants.

entries.name: Pervasive Goodwill
entries: At the end of each long rest taken on this plane, a visitor that is neither lawful good nor neutral good must make a DC 10 Wisdom saving throw.
On a failed save, the creature's alignment changes to lawful good or neutral good (whichever is closer to the creature's current alignment).
The change becomes permanent if the creature doesn't leave the plane within ld4 days. Otherwise, the creature's alignment reverts to normal after one day spent on a plane other than Bytopia.
Casting the {@spell Dispel Evil and Good|PHB.Spell.DispelEvilAndGood} spell on the creature also restores its original alignment.

entries.name: Elysium
entries.description: A plane of existence where creatures of unfettered kindness and compassion reside. Its bucolic landscapes glow with life and beauty, offering tranquility that seeps into the souls of travelers. A refuge for planar wanderers seeking safety, it serves as a heaven of well-earned rest where joy and serenity abound.

entries.name: Overwhelming Joy
entries.description: Visitors spending any time on this plane risk becoming trapped by overwhelming sensations of contentment and happiness. At the end of each long rest taken on this plane, a visitor must make a DC 10 Wisdom saving throw.
entries.effect: On a failed save, the creature is unwilling to leave the plane before taking another long rest. After three failed saving throws, the creature never willingly leaves the plane and, if forcibly removed, does everything in its power to return to the plane.
entries.removal: A dispel evil and good spell removes this effect from the creature.

entries.name: The Beastlands
entries: The Beastlands is a plane of nature unbound, featuring forests ranging from moss-hung mangroves to snow-laden pines, thick jungles where branches weave so tightly that no light penetrates, and vast plains where grains and wildflowers wave in the wind with vibrant life. This plane embodies nature's wildness and beauty, while also speaking to the animal within all living things.

entries.name: Hunter's Paradise
entries: Visitors to the Beastlands find their hunting and stalking capabilities improved, and characters have advantage on Wisdom (Animal Handling), Wisdom (Perception), and Wisdom (Survival) checks while there.

entries.name: Beast Transformation
entries.description: When a visitor slays a beast native to a plane, the slayer must succeed on a {@dc 10} Charisma saving throw or be transformed (as the {@spell Polymorph} spell) into the slain beast's type. The transformed creature retains its intelligence and ability to speak. At the end of each long rest, the creature can repeat the saving throw. On a success, it reverts to its true form. After three failed saving throws, the transformation can only be undone by a {@spell Remove Curse} spell or similar magic.

entries.text: Arborea is a plane of violent moods and deep affections, where passion and rage are as honored as joy. Its inhabitants are good-natured but prone to reckless emotions that can lead to devastating consequences. The plane features extravagantly massive and beautiful natural landscapes, with nature spirits inhabiting every glade and stream. Travelers must tread lightly due to the plane's volatile nature. Elves born here have the celestial type and are wild at heart, eager to battle evil.

entries.name: Intense Yearning
entries.description: When a visitor leaves Borea, they must make a Constitution saving throw against a DC of 5 + 1 per day spent on the plane. On failure, the creature gains a yearning to return to Borea. While affected, the creature has advantage on ability checks. At the end of each long rest, the creature can repeat the saving throw to end the effect on success. The spell *Dispel Evil and Good* can end this effect.

entries.name: Borea
entries.description: An outer plane associated with cold, winter, and desolation. Its inhabitants are bound to the harsh, frigid environment, and visitors may develop an intense yearning to return despite the dangers.
entries.lore: Borea is one of the Nine Hells' outer planes, though its exact nature varies across lore. It is often depicted as a frozen wasteland where time and space are distorted, and the cold is so extreme it can freeze even the most resilient creatures.

entries.name: Description
entries: Ysgard is a rugged realm of soaring mountains, deep valleys, and windswept battlefields, with summers that are long and hot, and winters that are wickedly cold and unforgiving. Its continents float above oceans of nic rock, below which are icy caverns so enormous they can hold entire kingdoms of giants, humans, dwarves, and other beings.

entries.name: Environment
entries: Soaring mountains
Deep valleys
Windswept battlefields
Oceans of nic rock
Icy caverns

entries.name: Inhabitants
entries: Giants
Humans
Dwarves
Other beings

entries.name: Challenges
entries: Heroes come to Ysgard to test their mettle not only against the plane itself, but also against giants, dragons, and other terrible creatures that roam across Ysgard's vast terrain.

entries.name: Ysgard
entries.description: The home of slain heroes who wage eternal battles on fields of glory. Any creature, other than a construct or undead, that is killed by an attack or effect while on Ysgard is restored to life at dawn the next day. The creature has all its hit points restored, and all injuries and afflictions it suffered before its death are erased.

rules.name: Immortal Wrath
rules.description: Creatures killed on Ysgard are resurrected at dawn with full hit points and healed of all injuries. This effect applies only to non-construct and non-undead creatures.

entries.name: Limbo
entries.description: A plane of pure chaos, a roiling soup of permanent matter and energy. Stone melts into metal, which freezes into diamond, which burns into smoke, and so on in an endless, unpredictable process of transformation. Fragments of ordinary landscapes drift through the disorder. The plane has no gravity, and creatures float in place.

rules.name: Mind-Driven Conformation
rules.description: Creatures can move up to their walking speed in any direction by merely thinking of the desired direction. Disciplined and powerful minds can create islands or structures within Limbo, while simple-minded creatures may have their pockets of space vanish or transform within seconds.

lore.name: Inhabitants of Limbo
lore.description: The slaadi live here and swim amid the chaos, creating nothing, whereas githzerai monks build entire monasteries with their minds.

entities.name: OPTIONAL RULE: POWER OF THE MIND

entries.name: Moving Objects on Limbo
entries: As an action, a creature on Limbo can make an Intelligence check to mentally move an object on the plane that it can see within 30 feet. {@dc 5} for Tiny, {@dc 10} for Small, {@dc 15} for Medium, {@dc 20} for Large, and {@dc 25} for Huge or larger.
On a successful check, the creature moves the object 5 feet plus 1 foot for every point by which it beat the DC.

entries.name: Altering Nonmagical Objects
entries: A creature can use an action to make an Intelligence check to alter a nonmagical object that isn't being worn or carried. {@dc 10} for Tiny, {@dc 15} for Small, {@dc 20} for Medium, and {@dc 25} for Large or larger.
On a success, the creature changes the object into another nonliving form of the same size (e.g., turning a boulder into a ball of fire).

entries.name: Stabilizing a Spherical Area
entries: A creature can use an action to make an Intelligence check to stabilize a spherical area centered on the creature. {@dc 5} for a 10-foot-radius sphere; each additional 10 feet increases the DC by 5.
On a successful check, the creature prevents the area from being altered by the plane for 24 hours, or until the creature uses this ability again.

entries.name: Environmental Effects
entries: Cold, noisy, and dark with no natural light
Nonmagical open flames (torches, campfires) are quickly extinguished by wind
Conversation requires yelling, effective only up to 10 feet
Creatures have disadvantage on ability checks relying on hearing

entries.name: Inhabitants
entries: Primarily creatures banished to the plane with no escape
Many driven mad by incessant winds or forced to take shelter in windless areas where sounds resemble distant cries of torment

entries.name: Mad Winds
entries.description: A visitor must make a DC 10 Wisdom saving throw after each hour spent among the howling winds. On a failed save, the creature gains one level of exhaustion. A creature that reaches six levels of exhaustion while on this plane doesn't die. Instead, the creature gains a random form of indefinite madness, as described in chapter 8, 'Running the Game.' Finishing a long rest doesn't reduce a creature's exhaustion level unless the creature can somehow escape the maddening winds.

entries.name: Abyss
entries.description: The Abyss embodies all that is perverse, gruesome, and chaotic. Its virtually endless layers spiral downward into ever more appalling forms. Each layer of the Abyss boasts its own horrific environment. Although no two layers are alike, they are all harsh and inhospitable. Each layer also reflects the entropic nature of the Abyss. In fact, much of what one sees or touches on the plane seems to be in a decaying, crumbling, or corroded state.

entries.name: Abyssal Corruption
entries.description: A non-evil visitor that finishes a long rest in the Abyss must make a DC 10 Charisma saving throw. On failure, the creature becomes corrupted. Refer to the Abyssal Corruption table to determine the effects of this corruption. You can substitute different corruption effects of your own creation.

entries.name: Ending Corruption
entries.description: After finishing a long rest, a corrupted creature can make a DC 15 Charisma saving throw. On a successful save, the corruption effect ends. A dispel evil and good spell or any magic that removes a curse also ends the effect.

entries.name: Alignment Change
entries.description: If a corrupted creature doesn't leave the plane within 1d4 + 2 days, its alignment changes to chaotic evil. Casting the dispel evil and good spell on the creature restores its original alignment.

entries.name: 1-4 Treachery
entries: The character gains the following flaw: "I can only achieve my goals by making sure that my companions don't achieve theirs."

entries.name: 5-7 Bloodlust
entries: The character gains the following flaw: "I enjoy killing for its own sake, and once I start, it's hard to stop."

entries.name: 8-9 Mad Ambition
entries: The character gains the following flaw: "I am destined to rule the Abyss, and my companions are tools to that end."

entries.name: 10 Demonic Possession
entries: The character is possessed by a demonic entity until freed by dispel evil and good or similar magic. Whenever the possessed character rolls a 1 on an attack roll, ability check, or saving throw, the demon takes control of the character and determines the character's behavior. At the end of each of the possessed character's turns, they can make a DC 15 Charisma saving throw. On a success, the character regains control until they roll another 1.

entries.name: The Gaping Maw
entries.description: A vast wilderness of savagery and madness where even powerful demons go insane with fear. It consists of a primeval continent covered in dense jungle, surrounded by an endless ocean and brine fiats. The Prince of Demons rules from two serpentine towers topped with fanged skulls, housing the fortress of Abysm.

entries.name: Thanatos
entries.description: A bleak realm under a black sky, featuring mountains, barren moors, ruined cities, and forests of twisted black trees. Tombs and undead swarm the landscape, with Orcus ruling from the obsidian-and-bone palace Everlost within the wasteland Oblivion's End.

entries.name: The Demonweb
entries.description: An immense network of magical webbing forming passageways and chambers. Buildings and objects hang suspended in the web, with random portals connecting to demiplanes and the Material Plane. Dungeons are built within the web, and the Demonweb Pits house Lolth's yochlol handmaidens.

entries.name: The Endless Maze
entries.description: A never-ending dungeon centered on Baphomet's ziggurat palace. The palace is surrounded by a mile-wide moat concealing submerged stairs and tunnels leading deeper into the fortress.

entries.name: The Triple Realm
entries.description: Three layers of the Abyss ruled by Graz'zt, encompassing the bustling city of Zelatar with its markets and pleasure palaces. The realm feigns civility but harbors deadly mazelike cities and serpent-branch forests.

entries.name: Death Dells
entries.description: A layer of ravines where creatures must hunt to survive. Plants require blood for their roots, and Yeenoghu's servants capture Material Plane creatures for the Gnoll Lord's realm.

entries.name: Carceri
entries: A plane of desolation and despair, Carceri serves as the model for all other prisons in existence. Its six layers form an oppressive, unyielding realm for traitors and backstabbers, featuring:
These layers create an environment of eternal torment, reflecting the moral decay and punishment of those imprisoned here.

entries.name: Prison Plane (Carceri)
entries.description: Leaving Carceri is extremely difficult. Magical efforts to leave the plane by any spell other than a wish simply fail. Gateways and portals that open onto the plane become one-way only. Secret ways out of the plane exist, but they are hidden and well guarded by traps and deadly monsters.

entries.name: Hades
entries: A gray land with an ashen sky, Hades is the destination of many souls unclaimed by the gods of the Upper Planes or the fiendish rulers of the Lower Planes. The layers of Hades are called the Three Glooms, a place devoid of joy, hope, or passion.
This gloom leaches away light and emotion, more than most visitors can endure. The 'Shadowfell Despair' rule earlier in the chapter can be used to represent a visitor's despair.

entries.name: Shadowfell Despair
entries: See the 'Shadowfell Despair' rule in the chapter.

entries.text: At the end of each long rest taken on the plane, a visitor must make a DC 10 Wisdom saving throw. On a failed save, the creature gains one level of exhaustion, which can't be removed while the creature remains in Hades. If the creature reaches six levels of exhaustion, it doesn't die. Instead, the creature permanently transforms into a larva, whereupon all levels of exhaustion afflicting the creature are removed.

A larva is a miserable fiend that retains the facial features of its previous form but has the body of a fat worm. A larva has only a few faint memories of its previous life and the statistics in the larva stat block.
Hades is crawling with larvae. Night hags, liches, and rakshasas harvest them for use in vile rituals. Other fiends like to feed on them.

entries.name: Description
entries: Gehenna is the plane of suspicion and greed. It is the birthplace of the yugoloths, which dwell here in great numbers. A volcanic mountain dominates each of the four layers of Gehenna, and lesser volcanic earth bergs drift in the air and smash into the greater mountains.

entries.name: Environment
entries: The rocky slopes make movement difficult and dangerous. The ground inclines at least 45 degrees almost everywhere. Steep cliffs and deep canyons present more challenging obstacles. Hazards include volcanic fissures that vent noxious fumes or searing flames.

entries.name: Inhabitants
entries: Fiends living here are among the greediest and most selfish in all the multiverse. The plane has no room for mercy or compassion.

entries.name: Crue Hindrance
entries.description: The plane's cruel nature makes it difficult for visitors to help one another. Whenever a visitor casts a spell with a beneficial effect, including a spell that restores hit points or removes a condition, the caster must first make a DC 10 Charisma saving throw. On a failed save, the spell fails, the spell slot is expended, and the action is wasted.

entries.name: Overview
entries: The Nine Hells of Baator inflame the imaginations of travelers, the greed of treasure seekers, and the battle fury of all moral creatures. It is the ultimate plane of law and evil and the epitome of premeditated cruelty.
The devils of the Nine Hells are bound to obey the laws of their superiors, but they rebel within their individual castes. Most undertake any plot, no matter how foul, to advance themselves.

entries.name: Hierarchy
entries: At the very top of the hierarchy is Asmodeus, who has yet to be bested. If he were to be vanquished, the victor would rule the plane in turn. Such is the law of the Nine Hells.

entries.name: Pervasive Evil
entries.description: Visitors to the Nine Hells must make a Wisdom saving throw at the end of each long rest. A failed DC 10 save changes a non-evil creature's alignment to lawful evil. The change becomes permanent if the creature remains on the plane for 1d4 days. Leaving the plane causes the alignment to revert after 1 day on another plane. The spell *dispel evil and good* can restore the original alignment.

entries.name: Trigger
entries: End of each long rest taken on the Nine Hells

entries.name: Action
entries: Non-evil creature must make a DC 10 Wisdom saving throw

entries.name: Effects
entries: On failed save: Alignment changes to lawful evil
If not leaving the plane within 1d4 days: Change becomes permanent
If leaving the plane: Alignment reverts to normal after 1 day on another plane
Casting *dispel evil and good* restores original alignment

items: Rocky wasteland with rivers of blood
Battlefields littered with weapons and bones

entries.name: Rulers
entries: Archduchess Zariel (replaces Bel, former advisor to Asmodeus)
Tiamat, Queen of Evil Dragons (prisoner under Asmodeus's contract)

entries.name: Notable Features
entries: Zariel's basalt citadel with incinerated corpses
Zariel's appearance as a charred angel with burning eyes

items: Labyrinth of canyons between iron-rich mountains

entries.name: Ruler
entries: Archduke Dispater (devilish manipulator with horns, tail, and cloven hoof)

entries.name: Notable Features
entries: Hub for devilish conspi ring and soul deals
Dispater's obsession with mortal schemes

entries.name: Notable Features
entries: Deadly environment for inhabitants
Mammon's status as a ruler

items: Fiery landscape with fortress city Abriymoch

entries.name: Notable Features
entries: Belial's role as a co-ruler
Fierna's status as a co-ruler

entries.name: Rulers
entries: Archduke Levistus (imprisoned)
Former ruler Geryon (dismissed by Asmodeus)

entries.name: Notable Features
entries: Glasya's citadel with collapsing architecture
Torture chambers beneath her palace

items: Baalzebul's rule as Lord of Flies

entries.name: Notable Features
entries: Baalzebul's cursed deals leading to calamity
Asmodeus's enigmatic favor toward him

entries.name: Notable Features
entries: Cities embedded in ice
Mephistopheles's cunning nature

entries.name: Nessus
entries: The lowest layer of the Nine Hells, Nessus is a realm of dark pits whose walls are set with fortresses. Pit fiend generals loyal to Asmodeus garrison their diabolical legions and plot the conquest of the multiverse. At the center of the layer stands a vast rift of unknown depth, from which rises the great citadel-spire of Malsheem, home to Asmodeus and his infernal court.

items: Malsheem resembles a gigantic hollowed-out stalagmite.
The citadel is a prison for souls locked away by Asmodeus for safekeeping.
Releasing even one soul from Malsheem requires a steep price, with rumors of the Archduke of Nessus claiming entire kingdoms for such favors.

entries: Asmodeus, the ruler of Malsheem, appears as a handsome, bearded humanoid with small horns, piercing red eyes, and flowing robes. He wields a ruby-tipped scepter and is renowned as the most cunning and well-mannered of archdevils.
Acheron is a plane of existence characterized by perpetual strife and war. Its four layers consist of enormous iron cubes floating in an airy void.
Collisions between the iron cubes produce echoes of past conflicts, which blend with the sounds of endless battles between spirits of fallen soldiers and various warlike factions.
The plane's nature revolves around combat, with armies of spirits fighting against orcs loyal to Gruumsh, goblinoids serving Maglubiyet, and legions under other war gods.

entries.name: Bloodlust
entries.description: Acheron rewards a creature for harming other creatures by imbuing that creature with the strength to keep fighting. While on Acheron, a creature gains temporary hit points equal to half its hit point maximum whenever it reduces a hostile creature to 0 hit points.

entries.name: Mechanus
entries: A realm of clockwork gears where law is reflected in a realm of interlocked and turning gears according to their measure. The cogs seem to be engaged in a calculation so vast that no deity can fathom its purpose. Mechanus embodies absolute order, and its influence can be felt on those who spend time here.
The plane is also home to the creator of the modrons: a godlike being called Primus.

entries.name: Law of Averages
entries.description: While on Mechanus, creatures always use the average damage result for attacks and spells. For example, an attack that normally deals 1d10 + 5 damage always deals 10 damage on Mechanus.

entries.name: Imposing Order
entries: At the end of each long rest taken on this plane, a visitor that isn't lawful neutral must make a DC 10 Wisdom saving throw. On a failed save, the creature's alignment changes to lawful neutral. The creature's alignment reverts to normal after one day spent on a plane other than Mechanus. Casting the dispel evil and good spell on the creature also restores its original alignment.

entries.name: Mechanus Overview
entries: Arcadia thrives with orchards of perfectly lined trees, ruler-straight streams, orderly fields, perfect roads, and cities laid out in geometrically pleasing shapes. The mountains are unblemished by erosion. Everything on Arcadia works toward the common good and a flawless form of existence. Here, purity is eternal, and nothing intrudes on harmony.

Night and day are determined by an orb that floats above Arcadia's highest peak. Half of the orb radiates sunlight and brings about the day; the other half sheds moonlight and brings on the starry night. The orb rotates evenly without fail, spreading day and night across the entire plane.

The weather in Arcadia is governed by four allied demigods called the Storm Kings: the Cloud King, the Wind Queen, the Lightning King, and the Rain Queen. Each one lives in a castle surrounded by the type of weather that king or queen controls.
Hidden below Arcadia's beautiful mountains are numerous dwarven kingdoms that have withstood the passage of millennia. Dwarves born on this plane are normal dwarves.

entries.name: Plane of Vitality
entries: While on this plane, creatures can't be frightened or poisoned, and they are immune to disease and poison.

entries.name: Description
entries: The Outlands is the plane between the Outer Planes, functioning as a plane of neutrality that paradoxically balances all aspects of the Outer Planes in a state of simultaneous concordance and opposition.
Its terrain varies, featuring prairies, mountains, and shallow rivers.

The plane is circular, resembling a great disk. This shape inspired the concept of the Great Wheel, though the argument is circular since the Outlands' vastness led to the idea of the Great Wheel in the first place.
The Outlands is a microcosm of the planes, containing a little of everything and serving as the closest the Outer Planes come to resembling a Material Plane.

entries.name: Settlements
entries: Sixteen te-towns encircle the plane's edge, each built around a portal to an Outer Plane. These settlements share characteristics of their connected planes, such as celestial and fiendish beings coexisting in a tavern.

entries.name: Gameplay Notes
entries: Campaigns set in the Outlands can avoid planar travel mechanics, as the plane's structure allows for seamless transitions between Outer Planes.

entries.name: The Seven Heavens of Mount Celestia
The Infinite Layers of the Abyss
Sigil, City of Doors
entries: A ring-shaped city in the Outlands, built on the inner rim of a floating ring. Its structures curve upward, allowing creatures to see the far side of the city directly overhead.
Known as the City of Doors, it contains countless portals to other planes and worlds, making it a major hub for planar trade and information exchange.
Sustains a brisk trade in portal keys, command words, and information about interplanar travel.
Ruled by the enigmatic Lady of Pain, whose true nature and intentions remain unknown to even her city's inhabitants.

entries.name: Demiplanes
entries: Demiplanes are extradimensional spaces that come into being by a variety of means and boast their own physical laws. Some are created by spells. Others exist naturally, as folds of reality pinched off from the rest of the multiverse. Theoretically, a plane shift spell can carry travelers to a demiplane, but the proper frequency required for the tuning fork would be extremely hard to acquire. The gate spell is more reliable, assuming the caster knows of the demiplane.

A demiplane can be as small as a single chamber or large enough to contain an entire realm. For example, a Mordenkainen's magnificent mansion spell creates a demiplane consisting of a foyer with multiple adjoining rooms, while the land of Barovia (in the Ravenloft setting) exists entirely within a demiplane under the sway of its vampire lord, Strahd von Zarovich. When a demiplane is connected to the Material Plane or some other plane, entering it can be as simple as stepping through a portal or passing through a wall of mist.

entries.text: The Far Realm is outside the known multiverse. In fact, it might be an entirely separate universe with its own physical and magical laws. Where stray energies from the Far Realm leak onto another plane, matter is warped into alien shapes that defy understandable geometry and biology. Aberrations such as mind flayers and beholders are either from this plane or shaped by its strange influence.

The entities that abide in the Far Realm itself are too alien for a normal mind to accept without strain. Titanic creatures swim through nothingness there, and unspeakable things whisper awful truths to those who dare listen. For mortals, knowledge of the Far, Realm is a struggle of the mind to overcome the boundaries of matter, space, and sanity. Some warlocks embrace this struggle by forming pacts with entities there. Anyone who has seen the Far Realm mutters about eyes, tentacles, and horror.

entries.name: Firestorm Peak
entries.description: Ancient elves once opened a vast portal to the Far Realm within this mountain, but their civilization imploded in bloody terror and the portal's location—even its home world—is long forgotten. Lost portals might still exist, marked by an alien magic that mutates the area around them.

entries: Some warlocks form pacts with entities from the Far Realm.

entities.name: KNOWN WORLDS OF THE MATERIAL PLANE

entries.name: Toril
entries.description: The heroic-fantasy world of the Forgotten Realms setting, featuring fantastic cities, ancient empires, and regions like Al-Qadim, Kara-Tur, and Maztica. Its dungeons are rich with history.

entries.name: Oerth
entries.description: The sword-and-sorcery world of the Greyhawk setting, home to the Free City of Greyhawk (a hub of scoundrels and archmagi) and the nightmarish realm of the evil demigod Iuz in the north.

entries.name: Krynn
entries.description: The epic-fantasy world of the Dragonlance setting, where the return of the gods is overshadowed by the evil dragon queen Takhisis and her dragons, plunging Ansalon into war.

entries.name: Athas
entries.description: The sword-and-sorcery world of the Dark Sun setting, a desert realm where water is more valuable than life, and sorcerer-kings rule as tyrants with scarce metal resources.

entries.name: Eberron
entries.description: The heroic-fantasy world of the Eberron setting, marked by a post-war cold war, magic as commonplace, dragonmarked houses rivaling kingdoms, and elemental vehicles enabling global travel.

entries.name: Aebrynis

entries.name: Mystara
entries.description: A heroic-fantasy world from early D&D editions, shaped by diverse cultures, savage monsters, warring empires, and the meddling of the Immortals—former adventurers raised to near-divine status.
